Polymers are the most favourable membrane materials for this process owing to their good mixed gas selectivity and controllablestructureandproperties,butwithcorrespondingrelatively low gas permeability nonetheless [6,7]. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polymers of intrinsic microporosity (PIMs) have attracted increasing interest in gas separation membranes due to their high permeability. However, the moderate selectivity limits their wide applications in CO2 separation. Herein we report a facile strategy to improve simultaneously the permeability and CO2 selectivity of PIMs membrane using the prepared PIM-1/graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets mixed matrix membrane. The GO nanosheets improves the hydrophilicity and surface roughness of PIM-1 membrane whereas the uniform assembly of GO nanosheets in the PIM-1 matrix creates the hydrophilic/hydrophobic microphase segregation in the membrane. The as-prepared mixed matrix membrane is also very porous and has a pore size of about 0.78 nm. The combination of these properties signi fi cantly contributes to the enhancement of the gas separation performance of PIM-1 membrane, with the resultant membrane exhibiting an exceptionally high CO2 permeability of up to 6169 barrer as well as a high CO2/N2 selectivity of 123.5, which is more than 7 times that of the pure PIM-1 membrane. The developed PIM-1/GO mixed matrix membrane should have a wide application in the CO2 separation.

texts.orig: With the intention of improving the gas selectivity of PIM-1 membrane, researchers developed several strategies such as thermal crosslinking, grafting of functional group, blending with other polymers, and solution mixing with nano fi llers [1,22 -26]. The grafting of functional groups with a special af fi nity for a target gas molecule can effectively improve the gas selectivity of the PIMs membranes. For example, Khan et al. demonstrated that bygrafting tri fl uoromethyl and phenylsulfone groups to PIM-1 chains, the CO2/N2 selectivity improved from 15 to 22 [25]. In another study, the carboxylation of PIM-1 increased the CO2/N2 selectivity from 11 to 26, but at the expense of decreased CO2 permeability from 8310 to 620 barrer [27]. Polymer blending on the other hand is effective by introducing new properties into the PIM-1 matrix. For instance, Yong et al. blended PIM-1 with the matrimid polymer to improve their CO2/N2 selectivity [28]. texts.label: text
texts.orig: The incorporation inorganic nano fi llers to form PIM-1 mixed matrix composite membranes represents the most feasible strategy to simultaneously improve the permeability and selectivity of gases and allow the composite membranes to surpass the 2008 upper bound. Incidentally, whereas the permeability are improved, the corresponding enhancements in selectivity are still small for the practical separations of industrially and environmentally important gases. A combination of controlled thermal oxidative crosslinking and fi lling with zeolitic imidazolate framework (ZIF-8) nanoparticles was shown to enhance the CO2 permeability from 2745 barrer to 3944 barrer, with decreased CO2/CH4 selectivity from 38 to 27 and a constant CO2/N2 selectivity of 27 [30]. Meanwhile, when silica nanoparticles are employed, the CO2 permeability is improved from 1200 barrer to 2615 barrer but with a drop in CO2/CH4 selectivity from 64 to 33 [26]. This kind of inherent ' trade-off ' relationship between permeability and selectivity of gas separation membrane therefore necessitates the continued exploration of more membrane design approaches with the capacity to achieve both high gas permeability and excellent CO2selectivity for the application of PIM1 based as well as other microporous polymers.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Currently, 2D nanosheets like graphene oxide (GO) that is only one carbon atom manometer thick represent a new class of advanced membrane materials from which gas transport nanochannels could be generated by their facile assembly into ultrathin fi lms and utilized for high performance gas separation [31 -33], photocatalysis [34], bioimaging [35] and conductive switching [36]. For example Li et al. reported the fabrication of the ultrathin GO nanosheets membrane with high selectivity of up to 3400 and 900 for H2/CO2 and H2/N2 mixtures while the coating of ultrathin GO nanosheets membrane on macroporous polyethersulfone membranes results in high CO2 permeability of  8500 barrer and CO2/N2 selectivity of  20 [37,38]. Likewise, in an effort to enhance the gas properties of GO membrane, Wang et al. recently reported that providing a suitable interlayer nanochannel spacing and physical microenvironments are conducive for the selective and facilitated fast transport of gas molecules [39]. GO nanosheets fi lled polymer nanocompositeis also shown toimprovegas separationperformanceofpolymermembranesliketheGOnanosheets fi lled poly(ether block amide) membrane developed by Shen et al, which exhibited the high CO2/N2 selectivity of 91, but with a low CO2 permeability of only 100 barrer [40]. Obviously, the GO nanosheets are promising membrane materials for CO2 capture, but it is challenging to produce GO nanosheets fi lled polymer membranes that are able to achieve both high CO2 selectivity and permeability.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this study, we demonstrate that controlled incorporation of the GO nanosheets can effectively tune the micropores in the PIM1 creating diffusion channels for excellent selectivity and permeability of gas molecules for application in CO2 capture. The as-prepared composite membrane achieves CO2/N2 selectivity as high as 123.5 with high CO2 permeability of 6168.9 barrer, and hence presents a great potential application in CO2 separation.

texts.orig: Preparation of the PIM-1/GO composite membrane
texts.text: Preparation of the PIM-1/GO composite membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The PIM-1 was synthesized from the monomers TTSBI and TFTPN with the potassiumcarbonate as the catalyst according to our previous work, adaptation of the low temperature Budd/McKeown method [1]. In brief, the equimolar amounts of TTSBI, TFTPN, and excessively potassium carbonate were added into a three-neck round-bottom fl ask with DMFas solvent, and reacted in N2atmospherewith constant magnetic stirring at 80  C for 48 h. The resulting mixture cooled to room temperature and then precipitated in methanol aqueous solution (1:1 v/v). The precipitate was puri fi ed by Soxhlet extraction in methanol for 12 h, and then dried in the vacuum oven at 80  C for 12 h to obtain the expected yellow powder of PIM-1.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The PIM-1/GO composite membrane was prepared via the typical solution casting and solvent evaporation process. Firstly, the PIM-1 powder (1  0.02 g) dissolved in the chloroform (20 mL) to form homogeneous PIM-1 solution and then fi ltered by a polytetra fl uoroethylene fi lter with 0.45 m m pores. Secondly, GO nanosheets dispersion was prepared by dispersing GO nanosheets powder (0.2  0.005 g) in chloroform (100 mL) by stirring for 12 h followed by ultrasonic treatment at below 35  C for 72 h. Finally, the GO dispersion (1 mL) was mixed into the PIM-1 solution (5 mL) under gentle stirring for 1 h followed by ultrasonic treatment for 4 h. The obtained uniformly dispersed solution was cast into a Te fl on folder and then dried in an oven at 35  C for 3 days for evaporation of chloroform [41]. The resulting PIM-1/GO mixed matrix membrane was peeled off and then dried in an oven at 35  C for another 12 h to remove any residual solvent.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (S-4800, Hitachi ) and atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Nanoscope-MultiMode/Dimension, Digital Instruments ) was used to observe morphology and microstructure of the membrane. The sample was prepared by freezing and cutting the membrane in liquid nitrogen for crosssectional SEM observation. The AFM image was obtained under the tapping mode at ambient condition (25  C, 65% relative humidity). FT-IR spectra was recorded on a Nicolet Avatar 330 spectrophotometer ( Thermo Electron Co. ). The hydrophilicity was measured by pendant drop method using a contact angle meter (DSA30, Kruss ) at room temperature. The thermostability was characterized by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) (TG 209 F1, NETZSCH-Gerätebau GmbH ) in N2 atmosphere. The surface area and porosimetry analyzer (ASAP 2020 HD88, Micrometrics Instrument Co. ) was used to measure sorption characteristics with respect to nitrogen at 77 K.

texts.orig: Formation of PIM-1/GO mixed matrix membrane
texts.text: Formation of PIM-1/GO mixed matrix membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The dense PIM-1 and PIM-1/GO mixed matrix membranes were prepared by the casting/solvent evaporation approach as depicted in Fig. 1. After a suf fi ciently long-time of stirring and lowtemperature ultrasonic treatment, the GO nanosheets were uniformly dispersed in chloroform and formed a dark dispersion containing 0.2 wt% GO (Fig. 1a). Similarly, the PIM-1 powders were dissolved in chloroform to form a yellow homogeneous solution of 5.0 wt% PIM-1 (Fig. 1b). The GO dispersion was mixed with the PIM-1 solution (1:5 v/v) under constant and gentle stirring to produce a uniform gray-like mixture (Fig. 1c), in which GO nanosheets are uniformly dispersed. Casting and drying resulted in the obtainment of the PIM-1/GO mixed matrix membrane (Fig. 1de). Obviously, the membrane exhibits the characteristic yellowish colour of the PIM-1, in which GO nanosheets are dispersed uniformly from the digital photos. The mixed matrix membrane is less transparent than the pure PIM-1 membrane due to the fi lling of GO nanosheets. Moreover, the composite membrane shows excellent fl exibility and toughness, and thus was expected to be stable during gas separation process.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Morphology of PIM-1/GO mixed matrix membrane
texts.text: Morphology of PIM-1/GO mixed matrix membrane

texts.label: text
texts.orig: SEM images of the membranes as shown in Fig. 2 clearly reveals that the PIM-1/GO mixed matrix membrane has the same smooth and homogeneous surface of the PIM-1 membrane at the nanoscale, just like most dense polymer membranes, suggesting the GO nanosheets uniformly dispersed and not aggregated in the membrane. In comparison, the PIM-1 membrane and the GO nanosheets fi lled membrane have similar inner microstructure from cross-sectional SEM images. The absence of aggregation of GO nanosheets also implied no phase separation in the membrane and con fi rms the uniform and successful dispersion of GO nanosheets into the membrane matrix and thus was expected to fully in fl uence their functionality for facilitated CO2 separation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: AFM was employed to further examine the morphology of the as-prepared membrane. As shown in Fig. 3, the PIM-1 membrane displays a smooth surface on a macroscopic scale with typical ridge and valley like topography of dense membranes (Fig. 3ab). The incorporation of GO nanosheets bestows the membrane with high mountain and pointed peak-like landform (Fig. 3de). As a result, the roughness of the mixed matrix membrane (5.10 nm) is higher than that of the PIM-1 membrane (1.20 nm). Furthermore, the AFM phase images of the membranes distinctively reveal the occurrence of microphase segregation in the composite membrane, which is lacking in the PIM-1 membrane (Fig. 3cf). These dark and bright regions respectively correspond to the hydrophilic domains of GO nanosheets and the hydrophobic domains of PIM-1 chains. This distinct morphology is thought to be induced by the remarkable incompatibility between the hydrophobic rigid polymer backbone and abundant hydrophilic oxygen-containing functional groups (epoxide, hydroxy, and carboxyl) in GO. The result is the development of gas transport channels at nanoscale that are well connected within the membrane matrix of the GO fi lled membrane, a structure that is favourable for gas sorption into the membrane and promote the enhancement of gas permeability.

Clearly, the characteristic absorption bands at 2240 cm � 1 and 1600 -1650 cm � 1 attributable to the nitrile groups and C ¼ C bonds of PIM-1 chains respectively are present in both PIM-1 its GO nanosheets fi lled membrane. Meanwhile, tiny absorption bands of carboxyl group ( � � COOH) are observed around 3200 -3800 cm � 1 and of C � � O bonds at  1640 cm � 1 for GO nanosheets in the spectrum of the GO fi lled membrane [42]. The absence of these bands in the PIM-1 membrane, suggests that the structure of the GO nanosheets was preserved in the as-prepared membrane. The calculated C-H/CN adsorption ratio from the FT-IR spectra is 10.74 and 14.26 for the PIM-1 and its GO fi lled membrane respectively. This con fi rms the introduction of the abundant � � CH groups of GO nanosheets.

texts.orig: Fig. 4b shows the thermostability of the membranes. The samples were heated to 100  C for 24 h under vacuum condition before the TGA measurement to obtain actual thermostability. The slight weight loss observed below 150  C, is ascribed to the evaporation of residual water, solvent and desorption of the CO2 in the membranes. A substantial weight loss is observed in the GO fi lled membrane due to the hydrophilic GO nanosheets that would increase the water molecules uptake by the membrane. The PIM-1 membrane displays excellent thermostability with the degradation temperature up to 450  C, while it is depressed to  400  C for the GO fi lled membrane owing to the introduction of GO nanosheets. In spite of this, the GO fi lled membrane still maintains adequate thermostability for the industrial application.

texts.orig: Nitrogen adsorption/desorption experiment was employed in order to examine the porosity of the as-prepared PIM-1/GO composite membrane in detail. Fig. 6 shows the curves of the volume adsorbed vs. the relative pressure ( p / p0 ). The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm at relatively low pressures, displays the characteristic steep nitrogen up taken, indicating the existence of small pores. The calculated BET surface area of composite membrane is 627 m 2 g � 1 , which is distinctly higher than the surface area of most porous polymer networks. Meanwhile, from the pore size distribution estimated based on the results of adsorption isotherms [14], it is found that the pore size for the composite membrane is about 0.78 nm (the inset fi gure of Fig. 6), which demonstrates the enhanced porosity of composite membrane with respect to the pure PIM-1 membrane [44].

Meanwhile, this membrane displays enhanced CO2 selectivity of 123.5, which is more than 7 times that of the pure PIM-1 membrane (Fig. 7b). This demonstrates a signi fi cant improvement in the trade-off relationship between permeability and selectivity that is existent in most of gas separation membranes [1]. It is known that the transport of penetrant molecules is generally described by solubility-diffusion mechanism in the gas separation process [3], and the GO nanosheets have a speci fi c adsorption af fi nity to CO2 molecules [38]. It is reported that the polar groups ( � � COOH and � � OH) have strong interactions with CO2 molecules, which facilitates CO2 sorption leading to the mixed matrix membrane displaying improved permeability while maintaining a remarkably high selectivity [39]. Additionally, AFM observed the hydrophilic/ hydrophobic heterogeneous interfaces in the GO fi lled membrane. The resultant occurrence of the microphase segregation creates additional gas transport channels, which favour the diffusion of gas molecules in the mixed matrix membrane [40]. Moreover, the new diffusion channels created through the interfacial defects between PIM-1 matrix and GO nanosheets all together promote the achievement of high CO2 permeability by the developed membrane.

texts.orig: Furthermore, we investigated the effect of heat treatment on the gas separation performance of the GO fi lled membrane. It is known that GO is easily reduced at elevated temperatures. Therefore, the composite membrane was heat treated in the oven at 150  C for 12 h in this study, in which the oxygen-containing groups were expected to be reduced and thus affected adsorption of CO2 into the membrane. Accordingly, the CO2 permeability decreases to 3503 barrer, representing a reduction of about 56.78% from the non-heated treated composite membrane (Fig. 7c). Surprisingly, this was accompanied by the slight increase in N2 permeability from 50 barrer to 173 barrer. Consequently, the overall CO2/N2 selectivity decreases by 16.4% to 20.2 after heat treatment. This suggests that the fractional free volume and subsequently, the gas transport channels connecting the free volume elements in the polymer matrix decrease. The degradation of oxygen-containing groups in the GO (Fig. 4b) affects the adsorption of the CO2 molecules. Meanwhile, due the formation of reduced GO nanosheets, the interface between GO nanosheets and PIM-1 matrix probably become more rigid and less permeable compared to the un-reduced GO with abundant functional groups

texts.orig: The good gas separation performance in our work can further be improved by employing a number of studied strategies in literature. For example, by the incorporation of metal oxide or metal iontreated GO (e.g. Zn 2+ , B4O7 2 � ), which enhance facilitated transport of CO2 leading to further enhancement of CO2 permeability [39]. Similarly, increasing the humidity conditions of operation could improve the performance of the PIM-1/GO membrane. Dry conditions have been demonstrated to lower gas permeance particularly due to some blocking of the CO2 transport due to the diminished channel size whereas the water vapour/water content correlates with high free volume inpolymeric membranes leading to a more selective transport of gas molecules [53].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Conclusions
texts.text: Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In conclusion, we demonstrate the preparation of a new gas separation membrane with both high CO2 permeability and selectivity by the incorporation of GO nanosheets in the PIM-1 polymer networks. The GO nanosheets are revealed to be dispersed uniformly in the PIM-1/GO membrane leading to a smooth surface with homogeneous inner-microstructure. The hydrophobicity and thermostability of the PIM-1 membrane decreases while the porosity of the composite membrane increases. The hydrophilic/hydrophobic heterogeneous interfaces in the GO fi lled membrane results in the occurrence of the microphase segregation, which creates additional transport channels for enhanced diffusion of gas molecules while the availability of speci fi c adsorption af fi nity groups facilitates CO2 sorption leading to the composite membrane displaying improved permeability and maintaining a remarkably high selectivity. Compared to the PIM-1 membrane, the composite membrane has excellent separation performance for CO2/N2, with a high CO2 permeability of up to 6169 barrer and CO2/N2 selectivity of 123.5. The developed PIM-1/GO mixed matrix membrane has a wide application in the CO2 capture and puri fi cation.
texts.text: In conclusion, we demonstrate the preparation of a new gas separation membrane with both high CO2 permeability and selectivity by the incorporation of GO nanosheets in the PIM-1 polymer networks. The GO nanosheets are revealed to be dispersed uniformly in the PIM-1/GO membrane leading to a smooth surface with homogeneous inner-microstructure. The hydrophobicity and thermostability of the PIM-1 membrane decreases while the porosity of the composite membrane increases. The hydrophilic/hydrophobic heterogeneous interfaces in the GO fi lled membrane results in the occurrence of the microphase segregation, which creates additional transport channels for enhanced diffusion of gas molecules while the availability of speci fi c adsorption af fi nity groups facilitates CO2 sorption leading to the composite membrane displaying improved permeability and maintaining a remarkably high selectivity. Compared to the PIM-1 membrane, the composite membrane has excellent separation performance for CO2/N2, with a high CO2 permeability of up to 6169 barrer and CO2/N2 selectivity of 123.5. The developed PIM-1/GO mixed matrix membrane has a wide application in the CO2 capture and puri fi cation.
